text:
The curtain has finally come down on a spectacularOlympic GamesinParis.

The French capital and its host of landmarks provided the perfect backdrop for events across the city during 16 days of sporting action.

Great Britain departed with an impressive hail of 65 medals (14 gold, 22 silver and 29 bronze), good enough for seventh in the medal table withUnited Statestopping the table ahead ofChina.

After an exhilarating Games filled with memories, controversy, triumph and heartbreak, we say ‘au revoir’ and look back on the moments that captivated and stirred controversy in the French capital.

Adam Peatywas one of Team GB’s early gold medal favourites but failed to defend his 100m breaststroke title – losing out to Italy’s Nicolo Martinenghiby an unbelievably narrow margin of 0.02 seconds.

Peaty was comforted by his family in the immediate aftermath, including his three-year-old son George, with the Olympian revealing the touching moment that left him – and us – in tears.

‘George normally goes, “Daddy, are you the fastest boy?”, and I would’ve said, “Not today”,’ Peaty explained.

‘But he says, “I love you, Daddy”, and that’s all I care about.’

After the despair of Tokyo three years ago, absolutely everyone was rooting forSimone Bilesto return to her sublime best this summer. She delivered.

Biles left Paris with three golds and a silver medalwith a series of stunning displays in her gymnastics events.

Celebrities flocked to watch Biles in action and after her Tokyo campaign was derailed by the ‘twisties’, she sealed the perfect redemption story.

Anthony Ammirati became a Paris legend without winning a medal as the video of the well-endowed pole vaulter went viral.

Roared on by a home crowd, Ammirati’s hopes of clinching a medal were deniedafter his bulge sent the bar flying while attempting to clear the 5.70m stageof his event.

While it was a moment of sporting heartbreak for him personally, Ammirati has thankfully seen the funny side, becoming a social media sensation in the process.

When it was confirmed the Olympics would beAndy Murray’s last hurrah, there was a feeling he wouldn’t be going quietly.

While there was no medal at the end of it, the Scot bowed out in typically exhilarating fashion alongside doubles partner Dan Evans, thrilling the crowdwith two dramatic victories at Roland Garros before a quarter-final exit.

While there was no fairytale ending, it was very much a departure on his terms as the British sporting legend said goodbye on the grandest stage.

Many who casually tuned into the air pistol shooting one Wednesday afternoonwere left convinced Turkey had ‘sent a hitman’ as part of their medal hunt, such was the poise and style of Yusuf Dikec.

Typically, pistol shooters compete wearing specialist eyewear to help improve accuracy and huge protectors in order to block out any distractions. Not Yusef, however.

Wearing his regular prescription glasses and a standard pair of earplugs, he effortlessly shot his way to silver as Olympic fans celebrated a new hero.

Just as the Olympic Games were drawing to a close, Australian breakdancer Raygun provided us with one more unforgettable moment.

Her routine stunned and delighted viewers in almost equal measure,but drew no love from event judges who did not reward her a single point.

‘Don’t be afraid to be different,’ she defiantly said on social media. ‘Go out there and represent yourself, you never know where that’s gonna take you.’

Perhaps the biggest story from the Paris Games surrounded Algeria’s Imane Khelif, whocelebrated a superb gold medal success on Friday night in her boxing event.

But her participation in the French capital over the last two weeks has been under intense scrutiny after she was disqualified from last year’s Women’s World Championships for allegedly failing International Boxing Association (IBA) gender eligibility rules.

The International Olympic Committee cleared Khelif to fight and has backed her inclusion throughout the Games.

The row intensified during Khelif’s fight against Angela Carini when the Italian quit after just 46 seconds, explaining post-fight she had ‘been hit so hard she couldn’t breathe.’ Carini later apologised to Khelif.

The filthy River Seine was a problem from the opening days of the Gameswith the triathlon postponed over pollution levels in the water– not ideal when you have a 1.5km swim ahead of you.

Triathletes were seen violently vomiting in the aftermath of what is already a torturous event. Belgium pulled its team from the mixed relay event after an athlete who previously swam in the river got sick, and a swimmer for Switzerland also missed the competition due to illness.

Millions were spent trying to clean the iconic stretch of water and French authorities were adamant there were no issues over the safety of the river with Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo even taking a dip to ease any concerns. But not everyone was convinced.

Cases of athletes being sent home for alleged bad behaviour was a theme throughout the Games.

Brazil’s Carolina Vieira was kicked out of the Olympics Villagefor allegedly sneaking off the premises with her boyfriend – later revealing she didn’t even get the chance to pack her bags.

Paraguay’sLuana Alonso was also removed from the village for ‘inappropriate conduct’shortly after missing out on a semi-final place in her swimming event. Reports also suggested Alonso snuck off the premises to squeeze in a quick trip to Disneyland rather than supporting her teammates.

Fresh after winning 100m, Noah Lyles returned to the track for the 200m finals two days later but appeared to be in some distress during the latter stages of that longer sprint, settling for bronze.

Moments after the athletes crossed the finish line, it emerged Lyles had tested positive for Covid ahead of the race and was later pictured being taken off the track in a wheelchair in the French capital.

Lyles revealed he had been feeling ‘really horrible’ two days before the final, leading many to question his decision to compete given the risks over mixing with fellow athletes in the lead-up.

BBC’s Gabby Logan described it as a ‘selfish decision’ with Michael Johnson ‘morally unsure’ over his participation – with Lyles also seen embracing teammate Kenny Bednarek who would later compete in the 4X100 relay race.

title: The most memorable and controversial moments of the Paris Olympics 2024 | Metro News
description: Imane Khelif, Luana Alonso, Adam Peaty, Simone Biles and Adam Murray feature in our round-up of the 2024 Olympic Games in Paris, France.
